A recent study suggests that targeted airway-control and breathing exercises may help improve respiratory efficiency, breath control, and physical endurance.

Researchers observed that participants who practiced specialized breathing techniques over several weeks showed improvements in measures of breathing performance and recovery following exercise.

The findings support the idea that the muscles involved in breathing can be trained, much like other muscles in the body. Improved breathing mechanics may benefit athletes, performers, and individuals looking to enhance endurance.

However, claims of dramatic increases in lung capacity should be interpreted cautiously, and more research is needed to confirm the long-term effects and compare these methods with established respiratory training techniques.

The takeaway: breathing is a skill that can be trained, and better breathing habits may contribute to improved performance and overall well-being.

Taare Zameen Par is a deeply moving Hindi drama released in 2007, directed by Aamir Khan, who also plays a pivotal role in the film. The story revolves around Ishaan Awasthi, a young boy who struggles academically due to dyslexia, a condition that makes reading and writing difficult for him. Misunderstood by his parents and teachers, Ishaan is labeled as lazy and troublesome, leading to his admission to a strict boarding school where his emotional state worsens. The narrative takes a transformative turn when Ram Shankar Nikumbh, an empathetic art teacher played by Aamir Khan, recognizes Ishaanâs condition and helps him rediscover his confidence and unique talents through patience, creativity, and understanding. The film beautifully highlights the importance of recognizing different learning abilities in children and criticizes the rigid education system that often overlooks individuality. With its heartfelt storytelling, emotional depth, and memorable music, Taare Zameen Par remains a powerful and inspiring film that encourages compassion, awareness, and acceptance toward children who learn differently.

A new behavioral psychology study has found that men experience a significantly stronger dopamine response when praised with the phrase âgood boyâ by a romantic partner than when receiving recognition in professional settings. Researchers at the University of Michigan observed that personal approval delivered in a trusted relationship carried substantially more emotional weight than workplace compliments.

The study followed 264 men over an eight-week period, monitoring dopamine activity, heart rate, and emotional response to different forms of praise. Participants were exposed to positive feedback from managers, coworkers, friends, and romantic partners. Researchers found that hearing âgood boyâ from a partner triggered a dopamine response nearly six times stronger than praise received during performance reviews or workplace recognition programs. Many participants also reported a longer-lasting boost in mood and motivation.

Researchers concluded that approval from emotionally significant individuals activates reward pathways more intensely than praise from authority figures or colleagues. The findings have contributed to growing research examining how relationships, affirmation, and emotional connection influence motivation and psychological well-being.

Deep within the Arctic archipelago of Svalbard, Norway built a facility designed to protect the future of global agriculture.

Known as the Svalbard Global Seed Vault, it contains backup copies of hundreds of thousands of crop varieties collected from countries across the planet.

The vault was placed inside a remote mountain where cold temperatures and thick rock provide natural protection for its contents.

If natural disasters, conflicts, or other crises ever threaten seed collections elsewhere, these stored samples can help preserve important genetic diversity for future generations.

What makes this remarkable is that the facility was created not for one nation, but for humanity as a whole.

Its purpose is to safeguard plants that people may depend on decades or even centuries from now.

Psychologists sometimes call this the public-private relationship gap. People often perform one version of themselves socially and another version in intimate relationships. 

Someone can joke about being âanti-men,â fiercely independent, or emotionally unavailable in public, yet become deeply nurturing, affectionate, and protective with a partner they trust.

Attachment theory also suggests that feeling emotionally safe can activate caregiving behaviours. We tend to soften around people who make us feel secure. 

The same person who argues online, rolls their eyes at romance, and claims they donât need anyone can suddenly become the one reminding their partner to eat, bringing them medicine when theyâre sick, and checking they got home safely.
